findings.title: gitleaks --verbose without --redact exposes secret values in terminal output
findings.description: The hook runs `gitleaks detect --staged --verbose --no-banner` without the `--redact` flag. When gitleaks finds a secret, --verbose prints the full match including the actual secret value to stdout/stderr. This means the raw secret value is printed in the terminal and potentially captured in shell logs, CI logs, or terminal history.
findings.impact: A developer who accidentally stages a secret and triggers the hook will see the secret value printed in plain text in their terminal. On CI systems that capture pre-commit output, the secret value would appear in build logs.
findings.recommendation:
Add the --redact flag so gitleaks replaces matched secret values with REDACTED in output:

  gitleaks detect --staged --verbose --no-banner --redact

This retains full diagnostic detail (rule name, file, line number) while keeping the actual secret value out of terminal output.

findings.title: Hook lacks explicit exit 0 on clean scan
findings.description: The hook body has no explicit `exit 0` after the gitleaks invocation. The script relies on the implicit exit code of the last command (gitleaks), which propagates correctly in sh. While this works in practice, it is less readable and harder to extend safely — any command added after the gitleaks line would silently become the exit condition.
findings.location: scripts/install-hooks.sh (hook body, end of EOF block)
findings.impact: No functional impact today. If the hook is extended (e.g., a log line added after the gitleaks call), the hook will silently exit 0 regardless of gitleaks result, bypassing the security gate.
findings.recommendation:
Add an explicit exit to make intent clear and safe for future edits:

  gitleaks detect --staged --verbose --no-banner --redact
  exit $?

findings.title: No .gitleaks.toml config file — relies on gitleaks defaults
findings.description: The hook invokes gitleaks with no --config flag, so gitleaks uses its built-in default ruleset. There is no .gitleaks.toml in the repository root. Without a project-specific config, there is no way to allowlist false positives (e.g., test fixture keys, example tokens in docs) or tune rules for the tech stack.
findings.impact: Developers may hit false-positive blocks on non-secret strings that match gitleaks default patterns, or may be tempted to bypass the hook entirely. Additionally, future gitleaks version upgrades could change default rules unexpectedly.
findings.recommendation:
Create a minimal `.gitleaks.toml` at the repository root to lock in explicit behavior, even if it only sets title and uses default rules:

  title = "gitleaks config for simple-sender"

  [extend]
  useDefault = true

Add allowlist entries as needed when false positives are encountered. Reference: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks#configuration
